senses.definition: A ray-finned fish belonging to the large group Actinopterygii, whose fins are supported by thin bony rays rather than fleshy lobes.
examples: The goldfish is an example of an actinopterygian found in many homes.
Most familiar freshwater species, such as trout and carp, are actinopterygian fishes.

senses.definition: Relating to or belonging to the ray-finned fishes of the group Actinopterygii.
examples: Actinopterygian fins are supported by slender rays of bone.
The researcher compared actinopterygian skeletons with those of lobe-finned fishes.

senses.definition: A major group or subclass of bony fishes with fins supported only by bony rays, including most living fish species.
examples: Actinopterygii is the largest group of vertebrates in terms of species number.
The class Actinopterygii includes both freshwater and marine fishes.

senses.definition: Having the characteristics of ray-finned fishes, especially fins supported by bony rays.
examples: The actinopterygious form of the fins allows efficient swimming in open water.
These fossils show clearly actinopterygious features in their fin structure.

senses.definition: The measurement or examination of the intensity of radiation, especially light or X-rays, using a special instrument.
examples: Actinoscopy was used to determine the exposure time for the early X-ray image.
In historical medical practice, actinoscopy helped doctors control the dose of radiation.

senses.definition: The main body of a radiate organism, from which ray-like structures extend.
examples: In some simple aquatic organisms, the actinosoma gives rise to many thin projections.
The central actinosoma is surrounded by numerous radiating arms.

senses.definition: A radiate body or structure with rays extending from a central point, especially in simple aquatic organisms.
examples: Under the microscope, the protozoan showed an actinosome with many delicate arms.
Each actinosome consisted of a central core and radiating filaments.

senses.definition: A genus of freshwater protozoa with a spherical body and many radiating pseudopodia.
examples: Actinosphaerium nucleofilum is often used in studies of cell structure and movement.
The pond sample contained several Actinosphaerium organisms with long radiating arms.

senses.definition: One of a series of small bones that support the fin rays in the paired fins of certain fishes.
examples: The pectoral fin is attached to the skeleton through several actinosts.
Each fin ray articulates with a separate actinost in the girdle.

senses.definition: A technique for creating or viewing three-dimensional images using radiant energy, such as X-rays or light.
examples: Actinostereoscopy allowed early researchers to examine internal structures in three dimensions.
The museum displayed actinostereoscopy images of historical medical cases.

senses.definition: Relating to the mouth of a radially symmetrical animal.
examples: The actinostomal region of the sea anemone is surrounded by tentacles.
Actinostomal structures are important for feeding in many echinoderms.

senses.definition: The mouth of a radially symmetrical animal, or the peristome in an echinoderm.
examples: Food particles are drawn into the actinostome by ciliary action.
The starfish uses tube feet around the actinostome to handle prey.

senses.definition: Relating to or using actinotherapy, especially the medical use of ultraviolet or similar radiation for treatment.
examples: Actinotherapeutic methods were developed to treat certain skin diseases.
The clinic offers various actinotherapeutic options for psoriasis.

senses.definition: The branch of medicine that studies and uses actinotherapy for treating disease.
examples: Advances in actinotherapeutics improved outcomes for patients with certain skin cancers.
She specialized in actinotherapeutics during her dermatology training.

senses.definition: Different kinds of medical treatment that use ultraviolet light or similar radiation to cure or control disease.
examples: Modern actinotherapies are carefully controlled to avoid skin damage.
Dermatologists may combine actinotherapies with topical medicines for better results.

senses.definition: Treatment of disease, especially skin disease, by controlled exposure to ultraviolet light or other active rays.
examples: Actinotherapy is often used to treat psoriasis and other chronic skin conditions.
Early forms of actinotherapy relied on artificial sources that copied sunlight.

senses.definition: Poisoning of the blood caused by toxic products formed under the influence of radiation.
examples: Severe overexposure led to actinotoxemia in the experimental animals.
Doctors monitored the patient closely for signs of actinotoxemia after high-dose therapy.

senses.definition: Any of the fine, hair-like fibers in the fin fold of an embryo fish that later fuse to form the bases of the fin rays.
examples: Each actinotrichium helps support the growing fin in the developing larva.
Researchers studied how actinotrichia contribute to fin growth and regeneration.

senses.definition: A free-swimming larval stage of certain marine worms, with a ring of cilia used for swimming and feeding.
examples: The actinotrocha eventually settles and transforms into the adult worm.
Plankton samples contained actinotrocha larvae among many other tiny organisms.

senses.definition: The uranium isotope with mass number 235, which can be split by slow neutrons and is used as a nuclear fuel.
examples: Actinouranium is critical for sustaining a chain reaction in many nuclear reactors.
Because of its fissile nature, actinouranium must be handled under strict safety rules.

senses.definition: Plural form of actinouranium, referring to samples or amounts of the uranium isotope with mass number 235.
examples: Different actinouraniums were tested for their behavior under reactor conditions.
The laboratory stored several actinouraniums in shielded containers.

senses.definition: An old taxonomic group once used for radially symmetrical marine animals like sea anemones and corals.
examples: In older classifications, sea anemones were placed in Actinozoa.
The term Actinozoa is largely historical and has been replaced in modern taxonomy.

senses.definition: Relating to actinozoa, especially to animals like sea anemones and corals in older classifications.
examples: The reef shows many actinozoal forms preserved as fossils.
Actinozoal structures are common in ancient marine rocks.

senses.definition: A radiate marine animal such as a sea anemone or coral, especially in older taxonomic use.
examples: Each actinozoan attaches to the sea floor and spreads its tentacles into the water.
The fossil bed was rich in actinozoan remains.

senses.definition: Relating to or characteristic of actinozoans such as sea anemones and corals.
examples: Actinozoan symmetry is typically radial with a central mouth.
The reef is dominated by actinozoan forms.

senses.definition: An individual actinozoan animal such as a single sea anemone or coral polyp.
examples: Each coral colony is made up of many tiny actinozoa, each called an actinozoon.
A single actinozoon can reproduce both sexually and asexually.

senses.definition: Plural of actin, the protein filaments that make up part of the cytoskeleton and help cells move and keep their shape.
examples: Actins interact with myosin to produce muscle contraction.
Different actins in the cell perform slightly different roles.

senses.definition: A small, tentacled larval form of certain cnidarians, especially hydroids.
examples: The actinula swims freely before attaching and developing into a polyp.
Each actinula is equipped with stinging cells for defense and feeding.

senses.definition: Plural of actinula, referring to multiple small tentacled larvae of certain cnidarians.
examples: Several actinulae were observed developing from a single hydroid colony.
The actinulae eventually settled on the substrate to form new polyps.

senses.definition: The process of doing something, especially to achieve a result or deal with a situation.
examples: The government must take action to reduce pollution.
After the fire, the community quickly took action to help the victims.

senses.definition: Exciting events in a story, film, or sports game.
examples: The film has a lot of action and special effects.
He prefers books with more action and less description.

senses.definition: A legal process in a court, especially to claim money or to make someone do something.
examples: The company brought an action against its former employee.
She is considering legal action for unfair dismissal.

senses.definition: The way something works or has an effect.
examples: The drug’s action on the nervous system is still not fully understood.
This chemical has a strong action on the metal surface.

senses.definition: The quality of being capable of leading to practical steps or legal proceedings.
examples: The report focuses on the actionability of its recommendations for policy makers.
Lawyers debated the actionability of the client’s complaints.

senses.definition: Providing enough clear information to be used as a basis for practical action.
examples: The survey results gave the team actionable insights into customer needs.
Managers need actionable data, not just high-level summaries.

senses.definition: Giving legal grounds for bringing a case to court.
examples: His remarks were considered potentially actionable under defamation law.
The behavior is unpleasant but not legally actionable.

senses.definition: In a way that can lead to practical steps or legal action.
examples: The feedback was not detailed enough to be actionably useful.
The statements were not actionably false, so the case was dismissed.

senses.definition: Relating to actions or events rather than states or conditions.
examples: Some verbs are described as actional because they refer to dynamic events.
In this theory, actional aspects of behavior are separated from emotional ones.

senses.definition: Relating to or concerned with action, especially in contrast to theory or thought.
examples: The organization promotes an actionary approach to social change.
Her role is more actionary than advisory in the project.

senses.definition: Carried out or completed, especially referring to a task or request.
examples: All customer complaints will be actioned within five working days.
The manager confirmed that the request had been actioned.

senses.definition: A film, book, or show that is full of exciting physical events such as fights or chases.
examples: His latest movie is a fast-paced actioner set in space.
Critics dismissed the film as just another mindless actioner.

senses.definition: Films, books, or shows that are full of exciting physical events such as fights or chases.
examples: He is known for directing big-budget actioners with spectacular stunts.
The festival will screen several classic actioners from the 1980s.

senses.definition: Plural of actio, a Latin term used in law for formal legal actions or proceedings.
examples: Roman law recognized various actiones to protect property rights.
The textbook lists the main civil actiones used in classical law.

senses.definition: Carrying out or doing something that has been requested or planned.
examples: We are currently actioning all outstanding orders.
The team is responsible for actioning the committee’s decisions.

senses.definition: A person who believes in or is strongly focused on taking action rather than only talking or thinking.
examples: As an actionist, she prefers doing something concrete to solve problems.
The group’s actionists organized regular campaigns in the streets.

senses.definition: People who believe in and are strongly focused on taking action, especially for social or political causes.
examples: The actionists pushed the organization to move beyond discussion.
Environmental actionists staged a peaceful protest outside the factory.

senses.definition: To turn a plan, idea, or requirement into specific actions.
examples: The manager’s job is to actionize the company’s strategy.
We need to actionize these recommendations into a clear project plan.

senses.definition: Turned from a plan or idea into specific practical actions.
examples: Most of the suggestions from the workshop have already been actionized.
The policy will be actionized through a series of pilot projects.

senses.definition: Turning plans, ideas, or requirements into specific practical actions.
examples: We are still actionizing the feedback from last quarter.
Actionizing the new strategy will require close cooperation between departments.

senses.definition: Not involving any activity or movement; lacking action.
examples: The committee remained actionless despite repeated warnings.
The first half of the film is almost completely actionless.

senses.definition: Things that someone does, especially when these things have a result or effect.
examples: His actions showed that he really cared about the team.
You must take responsibility for your actions.

senses.definition: Legal cases or processes in a court.
examples: Several actions were brought against the company after the accident.
Insurance can protect you from legal actions by customers.

senses.definition: Full of action, energetic, or inclined to act.
examples: The actious child could not sit still during the long lecture.
Her actious personality made her a natural leader in crises.

senses.definition: A proposed group of radiolarian protozoa characterized by radial symmetry and complex skeletons, used in older classifications.
examples: Actipylea were once separated from other radiolarians based on their skeletal structure.
The term Actipylea appears mainly in historical zoological literature.

senses.definition: The site in western Greece of a famous naval battle in 31 BC where Octavian defeated Antony and Cleopatra.
examples: The Battle of Actium marked the end of the Roman Republic.
Many historians see Actium as the key turning point in Octavian’s rise to power.

senses.definition: Able to be activated or made active.
examples: The device has an activable safety mode for emergency situations.
These settings are only activable after you enter the administrator password.

senses.definition: To make something start working or become active.
examples: Press this button to activate the alarm system.
You must call the company to activate your new credit card.

senses.definition: To cause a chemical or biological process to begin or become stronger.
examples: The enzyme is activated by the presence of calcium ions.
Heat can activate certain chemical reactions in the mixture.
